hero.title: LA Metro Safety Guide for Solo Travelers
hero.subtitle: A practical, scenario-driven guide to riding the LA Metro confidently — on the right lines, at the right times.

why_trust_this_guide.author_expertise: Built from real experience riding the LA Metro across the tourist-relevant lines — knowing which stations feel comfortable, which times are fine, and when rideshare is the smarter choice.
why_trust_this_guide.local_insights: Line- and station-specific notes based on layout, foot traffic, and time-of-day patterns on the B Line, E Line, and D Line corridors.
why_trust_this_guide.safety_framework: Structured around honest, proportionate assessments — telling you when the Metro is fine and when a different option is smarter, not blanket fear-based advice.
why_trust_this_guide.editorial_standards: Fact-checked, persona-driven, and updated regularly for accuracy.

core_principles.title: Stay Aware, Not Afraid
core_principles.description: The LA Metro is safer than its reputation. Confidence comes from understanding your line, station, and the time of day.

core_principles.title: Position Yourself Strategically
core_principles.description: Sit near other passengers rather than in empty cars, and keep to well-used, well-staffed stations.

core_principles.title: Trust Your Instincts
core_principles.description: If a car feels uncomfortable, move to another at the next station. Report issues to LAPD or Metro security directly.

core_principles.title: Minimize Exposure to Risk
core_principles.description: Avoid riding alone after 9pm, and pass through Skid Row–adjacent DTLA stations without lingering.

core_principles.title: Use Tools That Give You Control
core_principles.description: TAP card, transit apps, and rideshare let you stay ahead and switch to the smarter option when it makes sense.

metro_basics.how_the_system_works: The LA Metro is a mix of rail and bus serving a huge, spread-out city. For tourists the rail lines matter most — the B Line (Red) to Hollywood, the E Line (Expo) to Santa Monica, and the D Line (Purple) through Koreatown. Most of LA is more than a 10-minute walk from a station, so plan routes around the lines that reach your destination.

peak_vs_off_peak.peak: Daytime and early evening (before 9pm) are the lowest-risk windows. Trains are well-used and stations are busy, which adds a layer of comfort for visitors.
peak_vs_off_peak.off_peak: Late nights — trains get emptier and some stations have a visible homeless presence. After 9pm, rideshare is the better call on any line.

metro_basics.fare_basics: The LA Metro uses an honor system — tap your TAP card on the reader before boarding. Plain-clothes fare inspectors do check tickets and issue fines of $75–$250. Tourists are not exempt. Buy a day pass ($7) if you plan to ride more than twice in a day.
metro_basics.station_layouts: 7th Street/Metro Center and Union Station are busy, well-staffed hubs. Hollywood/Highland is well-patrolled due to tourist volume. Some DTLA stations adjacent to Skid Row, like Pershing Square and Civic Center/Grand Park, are less comfortable — pass through without lingering.
metro_basics.train_types: Rail lines are color- and letter-coded (B, D, E, and others). The B and E Lines are the most tourist-relevant; the D Line through Koreatown is also well-used and generally comfortable. Use Uber for anywhere off the main B and E corridors.

Keep your phone in your pocket rather than in your hand

Routes far from the B and E corridors

Stay near busy, well-staffed hubs like 7th/Metro and Union Station

Sit near other passengers
Use the emergency intercom at the end of the car
Exit at a busy, well-staffed station
scenarios.pro_tips: Trust your instincts — act early and report issues to LAPD or Metro security.

neighborhood_notes.peak_safety_notes: 7th Street/Metro Center and Union Station are busy, well-staffed hubs — safe at most hours.
neighborhood_notes.late_night_notes: Quieter after 9pm; rideshare is the better call.
neighborhood_notes.transfer_complexity: 7th/Metro Center is a major transfer point connecting several lines.
neighborhood_notes.avoid_times: Avoid lingering at Pershing Square and Civic Center/Grand Park, which are adjacent to Skid Row.

neighborhood_notes.peak_safety_notes: Hollywood/Highland is well-patrolled due to tourist volume.
neighborhood_notes.late_night_notes: Stays busier than most stations but still use rideshare alone after midnight.
neighborhood_notes.transfer_complexity: Simple — the B Line runs straight to Hollywood.

neighborhood_notes.peak_safety_notes: The E Line (Expo) to Santa Monica is among the safest and most tourist-relevant routes.
neighborhood_notes.late_night_notes: Comfortable in the evening; switch to rideshare late at night.
neighborhood_notes.transfer_complexity: Easy end-to-end ride; no complex transfers for most visitors.

faq.question: Is the LA Metro safe for tourists?
faq.answer: Yes during the day and early evening on the main tourist lines (B to Hollywood, E to Santa Monica). These lines are well-used and generally comfortable for visitors.

faq.question: Which LA Metro stations are safest?
faq.answer: 7th Street/Metro Center (DTLA), Union Station, and Hollywood/Highland are the busiest and best-staffed. Avoid lingering at Pershing Square or Civic Center stations adjacent to Skid Row.

faq.question: Is the LA Metro safe at night?
faq.answer: Less so after 9pm. Trains get emptier and some stations have increased homeless presence. Rideshare is the better choice after 9pm on any line.

faq.question: What should I do if I feel unsafe on the Metro?
faq.answer: Move to a different car at the next station. Use the emergency intercom at the end of each car to contact the operator. Call 911 if needed. Metro security number: (800) 371-5353.

faq.question: Do I need to pay fare on the LA Metro?
faq.answer: Yes — tap your TAP card before boarding. The Metro uses an honor system but plain-clothes fare inspectors do board and issue $75–250 fines. Buy a day pass ($7) if riding more than twice.

faq.question: Is the LA Metro safe for solo women?
faq.answer: Yes during daylight and early evening on the main tourist lines. Sit near other passengers, keep your phone in your pocket, and use rideshare after 9pm for the safest experience.

footer_cta.text: Plan your LA solo trip with confidence
